Architecture
The atomic unit of the System Graph is a finding composed of two nodes and a relationship
between them.
title: System Graph Technical Overview

text:
Unlike knowledge graphs that strictly relate concepts semantically, edges in the
System Graph are composed of structured statistical findings, such that a relationship in the
graph can be defined quantitatively, as opposed to semantically. The System Graph also
integrates mechanistic findings, following the same structured format of two entities and the
relationship between them. Unlike statistical findings, these findings are semantic in nature
(e.g., gene X activates biological process Y), but, like the statistical relationships, are based on
scientific findings extracted from studies and databases.. Nodes
All nodes or entities in the System Graph are represented primarily as variables. The variable
structure is flexible such that variables from a statistical finding, e.g. an independent and
dependent variable, or agents from a mechanistic finding, e.g., genes, proteins, molecules, &
biological processes, can be represented. The metadata associated with a variable includes its
definition, type, and any aliases or groundings to external identifiers. We describe below the
process for extracting this information.
title: System Graph: Integrating Statistical and Mechanistic Relationships

text:
The System Graph also contains two additional levels of hierarchy around each node or entity.
Variables can consist of multiple features extracted from datasets or machine learning models.
A feature is akin to a column in a dataset that can map to a variable in the graph. This allows
for the flexibility in the System Graph to integrate datasets and map columns in those datasets
to existing variables from the scientific literature. Variables are also mapped to higher-order
concepts. This allows for multiple variables referencing the same concept to be grouped
together (e.g.,
“type 1 diabetes incidence” and “risk of type 1 diabetes” matching to the
concept Type 1 Diabetes). This allows for the automatic creation of sets of concept-concept
edges in the graph for ease of traversability and discovery. We describe the process for entity
resolution in more detail below.
title: Hierarchical Integration of Variables in the System Graph

text:
U.S. and International Patents 2
Edges
All edges in the System Graph are composed of statistical and mechanistic findings from
verified sources, such as a scientific publication or an expert-curated database. The System
Graph currently handles over 50 statistic types including multiple types of coefficients, ratios,
and difference statistics (Supplemental File 1). For each statistical finding between two
variables, we extract and store the statistic type, statistic value, confidence interval, and
p-value, when available. We also extract metadata on the context for the relationship, including
the study population, study type and sample size. The System Graph also integrates over 20
mechanistic finding types commonly represented in biomedical knowledge graphs. These
include mechanisms like activation, inhibition, and modification between any two agents. For
each mechanistic finding, we store the mechanistic relationship type as well as various
metadata on the study or DOI including publication date and the source statement from which
the relationship was derived.
title: System Graph: Integrating Statistical and Mechanistic Findings

text:
Data extraction and integration
The findings in the System Graph are extracted at scale from the scientific literature (PubMed,
EuropePMC, OpenAlex) and expert-curated databases (GWAS Catalog, BioGrid, CTD) using
a suite of AI models and through integration with a host of expert-curated databases (Figure).
To the best of our knowledge, the System Graph is the first platform to extract and link millions
of statistical findings from the scientific literature at scale. To achieve this, our extraction
pipeline includes steps for ingesting and preprocessing scientific publications on a daily basis
and extracting and post-processing findings from these sources. This process is described in
greater detail below.
title: Revolutionizing Data Extraction and Integration in Scientific Research

text:
U.S. and International Patents 3
Extracting statistical findings from the scientific literature
We ingest scientific texts and rich metadata daily from corpuses such as PubMed,
EuropePMC and OpenAlex, as well as each new update of the GWAS catalog. These texts are
retrieved from each provider as large exports of structured XML, JSON, or CSV data. The data
is then parsed into relational databases and indexed on unique document identifiers such as
DOI. Text preprocessing involves a sequence of steps designed to enhance the accuracy and
efficiency of extracting statistical relationships from scientific texts using large language models
(LLMs). Initially, raw texts are segmented into sentences through rule-based sentence
boundary detectors. This step enables the selection of relevant sentences and the reduction of
longer texts. Following this, patterns and models were developed and trained on roughly 100
sample papers per tag and annotated. We created precise patterns to identify multiple types of
statistics and keywords, which indicate the presence of statistical associations.
title: Extracting Statistical Insights from Scientific Literature
category: Data Processing and Statistical Analysis in Scientific Research

text:
We then employ LLMs to extract structured statistical relationships from the pre-processed
texts. This process identifies two types of statistical associations: group comparisons and
effect size associations. Effect size associations detail associations that directly report a
measure of effect size (e.g. odds ratio, hazard ratio, relative risk, correlation) between variables,
often including a confidence interval and/or p-value for significance. Group comparisons
capture associations that do not provide an explicit measure of effect size, but rather compare
groups (e.g. means, medians, proportions) with confidence intervals and/or p-values for
significance testing.
title: Extracting Statistical Relationships with LLMs

text:
Additionally, we enhance the clarity and precision of our data by refining variable names,
making them more descriptive and closely aligned with the source text. Examples of this
include: spelling out any abbreviations based on their definitions in the source text and
specifying the comparator or control. This process not only improves the readability of our data
but also its fidelity to the original content, ensuring that our analysis is both accurate and
relevant. Through these follow-up processes, we ensure that our data extraction meets the
highest standards of quality and reliability, facilitating nuanced insights and robust analyses.
U.S. and International Patents 4
After the statistical findings are extracted, we implement rigorous data validation protocols to
guarantee that the extracted relationships adhere to predefined standards. This involves
verifying that the values are indeed found in the source text and ensuring the directional
accuracy of the relationship's effect.
title: Enhancing Data Clarity and Accuracy

text:
Integration of mechanistic findings into the System Graph
In addition to extracting statistical findings at scale using LLMs, we also integrate and layer in
mechanistic findings supported by the scientific literature. Mechanistic findings primarily
revolve around the interactions of genes, proteins, small molecules, and biological processes
that are not commonly studied statistically.
We extract and ingest mechanistic findings from three sources: directly from scientific articles
published on PubMed (via REACH reader), the BioGrid database [https://thebiogrid.org/], and
the Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD [https://ctdbase.org/about/], the latter two
being expert-curated. We use REACH to read and extract statements from new PubMed
abstracts daily, and monthly ingest CSV files of newly added expert-curated interactions from
BioGrid and CTD. We leverage the open-source INDRA software framework
[https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36938926/](Appendix D) to ingest and assemble mechanistic
data from various sources into a data structure that aligns with the System Graph.
title: Integrating Mechanistic Findings into the System Graph

text:
We consider statements supported by expert-curated databases to be accurate and suitable
for insertion to the System Graph. Statements exclusively extracted by the REACH reader from
literature, however, are prone to errors due to the precision of the REACH extractor. As such,
we apply a strict requirement that these statements must be supported by at least 15 findings
extracted by the REACH reader. These can come from one or multiple publications, and this
cutoff was chosen based on statement reliability analysis in [Bachman, et al]. This accuracy
filter is applied after the assembly process, and the resulting set of accurate statements is then
inserted into the System Graph.
title: Ensuring Statement Accuracy in the System Graph

text:
U.S. and International Patents 5
Integrating recommendations from clinical guidelines
In addition to extracting statistical and mechanistic findings, we also integrate
recommendations from clinical guidelines. We use human-curated data from Guideline Central
to integrate clinical guideline recommendations for more than 1,800 published guidelines from
all major societies. Guideline Central manually extracts recommendations from guidelines. New
guidelines are processed within 48 hours of being published and are directly integrated into our
database. Each recommendation is stored in our database with metadata corresponding to the
guideline name, authoring society, recommendation grade, etc.
Retrieving statistical relationships from datasets and ML models
While scientific publications provide the bulk of relationships in the System Graph to date,
datasets and machine learning models are integral sources of information from which to derive
novel insights about health from the individual to the population level. By layering these
sources into the System Graph, we can leverage these sources alongside extracted statistical
and mechanistic relationships from the peer-reviewed literature to create a richer accumulation
of health knowledge.
title: Integrating Clinical Guidelines and Data Insights

text:
To retrieve data directly from datasets, we have built connectors to data lakes and data
warehouses that read the data at the source and retrieve the metadata needed to integrate
these data with the larger System Graph. These metadata include things like feature names
(i.e., the name of each column in a dataset) and summary statistics for each feature. We also
enrich this data by calculating statistical relationships, including (but not limited to) appropriate
measures of correlation, between each of the features in a dataset, which, along with an entity
resolution process, allows us to connect these data to the larger System Graph. We also track
histories of descriptive statistics and feature-feature relationships to capture possible drift in
these measures over time. We developed our dataset retrieval architecture to ensure that none
of the raw data from the underlying dataset, especially no personally identifiable information, is
stored in the System Graph or even reaches our API.
title: Data Retrieval and Integration Architecture

text:
U.S. and International Patents 6
Outputs from machine learning (ML) models provide another source of statistical information
for the System Graph. We developed a suite of connectors to ML models built in different
machine learning environments (including AWS SageMaker, Databricks, Mode Analytics, and
different ML platforms within Google Cloud Platform). As with datasets, metadata to accurately
describe ML models are read from the environment and written to the System Graph. ML
model metadata can include training dataset metadata (if the dataset is specified), measures of
models’ statistical performance, as well as feature contributions to ML model targets as
specified by the trained model (when appropriate, measures like permutation scores, SHAP
values, model coefficients, etc.).
title: Integrating Machine Learning Outputs into the System Graph

text:
The connectors allow a user to quickly integrate the features,
outputs, and findings from their ML models into the System Graph, for instance, to measure the
relative predictive power of specific features to targets across different ML models or across
different test datasets. For each model, we also enrich the data by calculating and ranking
features by feature importance score, and, for models that are rerun on a recurring basis,
calculating potential statistical drift in feature metadata, measures of feature contributions, or
model performance.. Retrieving information on population and context
For each scientific publication from which we extract one or more statistical findings, we also
extract data on the population and context for the study. To do this, we use a combination of
LLMs to extract both structured and unstructured data on study population characteristics
such as sample size, demographic information, study location, and study time period, all of
which is stored with each DOI.
title: Integrating ML Insights and Study Context
category: Data Integration and Analysis in Machine Learning

text:
U.S. and International Patents 7
Entity resolution and categorization
Variable grounding and matching
In order to maximize reusability of the nodes in the System Graph and ensure interoperability
with external datasets and systems, we use entity resolution models across multiple levels of
our data to map each variable to a set of external identifiers, concepts, and categories (Figure).
For statistical findings, each variable in a relationship is processed through a semantic search
index of vectorized concepts pulled from a set of UMLS vocabularies (Table). To match
extracted variables to UMLS concepts, we employ a combination of keyphrase extraction and
semantic textual similarity techniques. First, we preprocess the variable names by removing
any population-specific text that may obfuscate the specific concepts of focus. Then, we
generate keyphrases for each variable using the KeyBERT framework {ref} and a PubMedBERT
model, with a minimum threshold of 0.5. This allows us to capture multiple components,
concepts, or ideas within a single variable. As part of this process, each keyphrase is assigned
a keyphrase
_
score that indicates its representativeness of the overall variable.
title: Enhancing Data Interoperability through Entity Resolution

text:
UMLS Vocabulary Last Updated
CPT - Current Procedural Terminology 2025
CPT in HCPCS 2025
HL7 Version 3.0 2025
U.S. and International Patents 8
LOINC 2025
MeSH 2025
NCI Thesaurus 2025
RXNORM 2025
SNOMED CT, US Edition 2025
HUGO Gene Nomenclature Committee 2025
International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision, Clinical
Modification
2025
ICD-10 Procedure Coding System 2025
Next, we perform a semantic search against UMLS concept names for all keyphrases, using a
search index, consisting of embeddings generated for UMLS concept names using a
pre-trained PubMedBERT model for semantic textual similarity. We retrieve all concepts with a
similarity of at least 0.5, and the similarity score is referred to as the search
_
score.
title: UMLS Vocabulary Updates 2025

text:
To rank and
score the matches, we combine all keyphrases and their corresponding UMLS candidates for
each variable. We calculate a raw
_
composite
_
score as the product of the search
_
score and the
keyphrase
_
score. Candidates are filtered using distinct thresholds for both raw and scaled (0,
1) versions of the search, keyphrase, and composite scores. These filters ensure that the
keyphrases are representative of variables, that the UMLS concepts are representative of the
keyphrases, and only high-quality concepts are surfaced..
title: Ranking and Scoring Keyphrases with UMLS Candidates

text:
Finally, we filter the results by a scaled composite score threshold of 87.5%, which provides
the highest concept matching coverage while maintaining high precision standards. To
consider a variable grounded, we identify all matches to stored UMLS concepts with a
semantic score of 0.99 or higher. These external groundings are stored as metadata at the
variable level. For each concept match, we also crosswalk the UMLS ID to Wikidata, which
crosswalks to multiple ontologies both within and outside of the biomedical and health
domains. The crosswalked Wikidata concept and ID is stored as a concept for each variable.
U.S. and International Patents 9
Concept roles and categorization
For each concept in the System Graph, we assign one or more roles and one or more
categories for data interpretability and aggregation (Table).
title: Filtering and Crosswalking UMLS Concepts for Enhanced Data Interpretability

text:
We assign categories for all concepts in the System Graph. We frame the problem as a
two-step classification problem, where the first step is to classify determinant, intervention,
and/or outcome, and the second step is to classify concepts under each main role into
corresponding subcategories.
Graph traversal and filters
Once we have extracted findings from source data and resolved the nodes, we then organize
and store these relationships in a graph database to be efficiently traversed and queried. Our
graph database comprises several key nodes:
●
Concept Node: Contains the concept name and a unique system identifier (system
_
id).
title: Two-Step Classification in System Graphs
category: Data Classification and Graph Database Management

text:
●
System Relationship: This node connects two concepts by linking their unique
identifiers (system
_
id) to form a new unique relationship ID. This node also includes a
bidirectional flag, which indicates whether the relationship between the two concepts is

bidirectional. The determination of this flag is based on the statistical types contained in
the findings for that relationship.
title: Connecting Concepts: Bidirectional Relationships in Health Outcomes

text:
Filtering the Graph
Lastly, each relationship in the System Graph is evaluated against a set of criteria, which is
then stored alongside the extracted findings and metadata as potential filters on the
relationships (Table).
Filter name Criteria Applies to
Well-established The number of sources supporting the edge or
relationship is within the top 10% of all other
relationships in the System Graph.
Statistical findings
Highly significant The number of statistically significant findings
supporting the edge is within the top 10% of all other
relationships in the System Graph.
Statistical findings
Highly cited The relationship is supported by at least one finding with
a cited by count within the top 10% of all cited by
counts in the System Graph.
title: Graph Relationship Filtering

text:
Statistical findings
Median effect size The median effect size across all of the statistical
relationships supporting that relationship is at least
small, at least moderate, or large.
Statistical findings
Sample size The relationship is supported by at least one finding
derived from a study with a sample size of at least 100,
at least 1000, or at least 10,000 participants.
Statistical findings
Relationship Sign The direction of the relationship between two nodes is
either positive (when measures of one node increase,
measures of the other also increase), negative
(increases are associated with decreases), or mixed.
Statistical findings
Established mechanism The relationship is supported by 15 or more findings or
extracted from a expert-curated database.
title: Key Statistical Insights

text:
Mechanistic findings
Recency The relationship is supported by at least one source
published in the past year or past five years.
Mechanistic and
statistical findings
Mechanism type The relationship is supported by at least one of the
following mechanism types: activation, inhibition,
increase amount, decrease amount, complex, or
modification.
Mechanistic findings
U.S. and International Patents 11
In order to calculate median effect size, we use well-established standards from the
biostatistics literature to convert statistical values to their effect size equivalents. Because the
sign of the effect depends on the definitions of variables made by individual research teams,
we take the absolute value of this effect size as the measure of strength of the relationship
between the two concepts of interest. In order to minimize potential statistical variation due to
outlier values or results from studies with small sample sizes, we apply the filter for relative
statistical strength on the median of these (absolute) effect sizes.
title: Mechanistic and Statistical Relationship Findings

text:
Clinical Graph
System’s Clinical Graph is a graph of clinically-relevant evidence derived from the scientific
literature, expert-curated databases, clinical guidelines and real-world evidence built to be
interoperable with electronic health records (EHRs) and power clinical decision support
solutions (CDSS). Evidence sources include PubMed, EuropePMC, Guideline Central, the
GWAS Catalog, BioGrid, and the Comparative Toxigenomics Database (CTD). APIs for
interacting with the Clinical Graph are optimized for clinical use; Clinical Graph nodes are
clinically relevant concepts built on codes from a number of medical vocabulary standards
(SNOMED CT, ICD10/ICD9, LOINC, CPT, HCPCS, RxNORM, NDC, among others). Retrieval or
search for nodes and relationships in the Clinical Graph is conducted with terms and codes
familiar to clinicians. This allows for interoperability between EHR platforms and the Clinical
Graph. Concepts in the clinical graph are also categorized according to clinical use (diagnoses,
conditions, lab tests, etc.) for use in clinical decision support.
title: Interoperable Clinical Graph for Decision Support

text:
Clinical Decision APIs
System’s Assemble API integrates patient data from EHR platforms with the Clinical Graph. For
a given patient, the Assemble API returns a unique instance of the Clinical Graph (a Patient
Graph), which serves as the foundation for personalized CDS. To create each unique instance,
the API processes each patient record to extract structured data corresponding to a patient’s
conditions, test results, medical history, active or recent medications or therapies, reported
SDOH, and other data domains relevant to clinical decision making, in accordance with HIPAA
Safe Harbor standards. The Assemble API returns the ranked list of relevant and important
codes and values for that patient. The Assemble API also surfaces the evidence-based
U.S. and International Patents 12
relationships between each of the clinical concepts reported within a patient’s record, and links
these to other concepts contained within the Clinical Graph for which patient data has not yet
been captured in the EHR.
title: Personalized Clinical Decision Support via Assemble API

text:
System’s Prevent API leverages a patient’s unique instance of the Clinical Graph (the “Patient
Graph” from Assemble) to algorithmically identify and summarize personalized behavioral and
lifestyle recommendations for disease prevention. The Prevent API delivers explainable
recommendations from the evidence in the Clinical Graph, specific to the patient’s
demographics, abnormal lab tests, known risk factors, and medical history.
System’s Synthesize API is a proprietary Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)-based
clinical question answering service based on evidence stored in the Clinical Graph. Clinicians
submit natural language queries and a novel AI agent-based retrieval and rerank algorithm
retrieves a set of comprehensive guideline and evidence statements from System’s Clinical
Graph to generate accurate, comprehensive, and up-to-date answers for each query. Each
statement in the response is fully cited and each response is assessed for evidence quality and
quantity, which is provided as part of the synthesis response.
title: Personalized Disease Prevention and Clinical Query Solutions

text:
The agentic process incorporates several architectural improvements on standard RAG
approaches to outperform on evidence retrieval; for one, unlike vector search retrieval from
snippets of extracted text, we used stored statistical relationships from the Clinical Graph to
ensure robustness of our answers to variations in query structure and wording.
The Synthesize API also works with the Assemble API's output to contextualize the evidence
based answers based on the entirety of the patient's record. Clinicians can ask questions about
potential diagnoses and treatment plans and be confident that the response from the Synthesize
API factors in the most up to date guidelines as well as all relevant demographic factors, active
diagnoses, biomarkers, requested medications, and social determinants of health.
title: Enhanced Evidence Retrieval in Clinical Decision-Making

text:
Uncovering the System of Cardiovascular Disease
Zoë Rogers
June 5, 2024

Cardiovascular diseases are one of the most burdensome health threats globally, accounting for nearly 20 million lives lost each year1
title: Understanding Cardiovascular Disease

text: One of the major challenges in addressing cardiovascular diseases is the complex range of factors that can lead to or exacerbate someone’s risk
title: Complex Factors in Cardiovascular Disease Risk

text: These include natural and built environments, social and economic contexts, genetic factors, individual behaviors, and pre-existing conditions
title: Factors Influencing Health

text: Each of these factors not only affect cardiovascular disease but are also interrelated and context dependent
title: Interrelated Factors in Cardiovascular Disease

text: In other words, cardiovascular diseases are a complex system
title: Complexity of Cardiovascular Diseases

text: Those confronting a complex system, e
title: Navigating Complexity

title: Return Process

text: , country or city governments, policy makers, health systems, healthcare workers, and NGOs, often struggle to identify where to intervene to have the greatest impact
title: Targeting Effective Interventions in Healthcare

text: A lot of articles have been published on the impact of individual risk factors, e
title: Impact of Individual Risk Factors

text: g
keywords: Please provide the text from which you would like to extract keywords.
title: Return Policy

text: , income, on cardiovascular disease, but there are few tools that inform the prioritization across multiple interventions to maximize health
title: Maximizing Health Interventions for Cardiovascular Disease

text: Resources for interventions are limited, and optimizing their allocation requires an understanding of the entire system and the key leverage points within that system
title: Optimizing Resource Allocation in Interventions

text: This is both time-intensive and methodologically challenging
title: Challenges of Time-Intensive Methods

text: The Novartis Foundation has started the AI4HealthyCities initiative to identify the key drivers of cardiovascular diseases and enable city governments to prioritize interventions with a potential for maximum efficacy
title: AI4HealthyCities: Tackling Cardiovascular Diseases

text: Collaborators in the initiative are already analyzing administrative, survey and other data sources
title: Data Analysis by Collaborators

text: These efforts are needed to understand the local context and systems of cardiovascular disease specifically, but this requires multidisciplinary teams combing through mounds of data over many months to create a shared understanding of the system of a single disease or set of diseases
title: Multidisciplinary Insights into Cardiovascular Disease

text:
But what if we could leverage advances in AI to generate a systemic understanding and representation of cardiovascular diseases, instantly leveraging the entire corpus of peer-reviewed scientific evidence? We set up a collaboration between System and the Novartis Foundation to do just that, and answer the question: 

What are the most important social and environmental determinants of stroke, acute myocardial infarction (AMI), heart failure (HF), and end-stage renal disease (ESRD), and what roles do diabetes and hypertension play as potential mediators?

Health is a complex system, yet health knowledge is siloed
title: Leveraging AI for Cardiovascular Disease Insights

text: We only ever see a part of the whole, impeding research and care
title: Partial Perspectives Hinder Progress

text: At System, we think there’s a better way
title: A Better Way at System

text: What if, instead, knowledge was organized as one interconnected system? We’ve developed an architecture based on systems not on silos, that’s only possible today because of AI
title: Interconnected Knowledge Architecture

text:
– Adam Bly, System Founder and CEO

The System Graph consists of millions of statistical findings extracted using AI from scientific studies and expert-curated databases
title: AI-Driven System Graph: Insights from Millions of Studies

text: This process runs daily on all newly published articles to ensure that the latest evidence is captured and represented
title: Daily Evidence Capture for New Articles

text: Once extracted, findings are assessed for accuracy, mapped to common ontologies, normalized, and linked together in a large-scale graph
title: Data Integration and Mapping Process

text: For example, the relationship between diabetes and stroke in the System Graph is backed up by 553 extracted statistical findings
title: Diabetes and Stroke: Statistical Insights

text: We can also use the System Graph to start to identify potential causal pathways from the literature
title: Identifying Causal Pathways with System Graph

text:
For example:
‍

The System Graph also categorizes millions of entities into a framework that includes biological, behavioral, environmental, and social determinants, all of which contain subcategories based on external sources, including AHRQ, CDC, and WHO
title: Categorization of Entities in the System Graph

text: Finally, similar to a meta-analysis or systematic review, evidence is filtered down based on quality, strength, and recency
title: Evidence Quality Assessment

text: For example, in certain analyses with the Novartis Foundation, the CVD system was filtered to only include relationships supported by a high number of sources, backed by a high number of significant findings, supported by one or more highly cited papers, with a median effect size that it is at least small (Cohen’s d > 0
title: Rigorous Filtering in CVD Analyses

text: 2) and supported by evidence published within the past five years
title: Recent Evidence Support

text:
Visualization of key social / environmental drivers of stroke
What we found
From a starting set of 2,513 determinants of HF, ESRD, Stroke, and AMI, a final list of 14 determinants was identified based on a number of studies supporting the impact of those determinants on one of our target outcomes
title: Key Determinants of Stroke Visualization

text: The results included environmental determinants such as air pollution, particulate matter, nitrogen dioxide, heavy metal exposure, noise level, temperature, and tobacco smoke exposure, and social determinants such as education level, employment status, socioeconomic status, income, neighborhood characteristics, geographic area, and marital status
title: Environmental and Social Determinants of Health

text:
‍

We also leveraged the System Graph to identify the most important mediators of these determinants and the various disease outcomes
title: Identifying Key Mediators in Disease Outcomes

text: Across the 14 key social and environmental determinants, there were many common biological mediators, including diabetes, hypertension, depression, metabolic syndrome, COPD and BMI, with diabetes, hypertension, and depression being the most common
title: Common Biological Mediators in Social and Environmental Determinants

text: Further, many of the social determinants, unlike the environmental determinants, were also mediated by behavioral and social factors
title: Mediating Factors in Social Determinants

text: For example, the relationship between education, employment, socioeconomic status, income, neighborhood characteristics and stroke were all mediated by behaviors such as smoking, alcohol consumption, physical activity, diet, and sleep, while education level and employment status were also mediated by insurance status
title: Influences on Stroke: Mediating Factors in Health Behaviors

text: In our AI4HealthyCities initiative, we aim to identify and address the key drivers of cardiovascular health to improve outcomes and inequities
title: AI4HealthyCities: Enhancing Cardiovascular Health Equity

text: System helps us to quickly synthesize vast amounts of published insights and understand and quantify relationships between determinants and outcomes
title: Rapid Insight Synthesis System

text: In combination with local data, this provides vital information to develop population health roadmaps
title: Developing Population Health Roadmaps

text:
– Peter Speyer, Head of Data and Analytics, Novartis Foundation
‍
Key takeaways
Leveraging the System Graph, System and The Novartis Foundation identified 14 key social and environmental determinants of stroke, AMI, HF, and ESRD and potentially important mediating paths
title: Identifying Key Determinants of Cardiovascular Health

text: The scale of the System Graph enables rapid identification of targets for intervention to improve health outcomes
title: Targeting Health Interventions with System Graph

text: Ultimately, this work will be used alongside other efforts, like the work being done across multiple city populations by the AI4HealthyCities team, to guide decision making over how best to allocate limited resources to improve cardiovascular health
title: Optimizing Cardiovascular Health Resources

text: The success of this project demonstrates how healthcare companies, governments, public health organizations, and foundations can leverage System to develop a systems understanding of any disease at unprecedented speed and scale
title: Revolutionizing Disease Understanding in Healthcare

text: Cardiovascular diseases are one of the most burdensome health threats globally, accounting for nearly 20 million lives lost each year
title: Global Impact of Cardiovascular Diseases

text: One of the major challenges in addressing cardiovascular diseases is the complex range of factors that can lead to or exacerbate someone’s risk
title: Complex Factors in Cardiovascular Disease Risk

text: These include natural and built environments, social and economic contexts, genetic factors, individual behaviors, and pre-existing conditions
title: Factors Influencing Health

text: Each of these factors not only affect cardiovascular disease but are also interrelated and context dependent
title: Interrelated Factors in Cardiovascular Disease

text:
Modeling the world as one interconnected system
The System Graph leverages state of the art LLMs, advanced graph technology, complexity science, and causal inference techniques to generate a systems model of the world — unifying scales and domains to connect siloed knowledge
title: Unified Systems Modeling
